item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m curious why a low-cost cryonics initiative is considered an idea which should never have been carried out. It sounds like a good idea to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This work, written by an unknown scholar, consisted of a discussion between eight characters citing the works of Aristotle, Thomas Aquinas, Avicenna, and several lesser-known figures in philosophical thought, making an argument for the moral imperative of suicide. The work concludes with each character committing suicide in turn. The scholarly citations and style of writing suggests that the work was considered during the ninth century AD by an author residing in Moorish Cordoba, making this entry the oldest observed unimplemented concept found within SCP-2303 to date.

Both Thomas Aquinas and Avicenna were born after the ninth century AD. How could characters have cited works that did not exist yet? Is this an intentional anachronism, or simply a mistake? (Also, AD should probably be CE.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's probably a whole library in there of failed SCPs. I should know, I've cluttered this site with enough trash!

What if malfunctioning anomalous items appeared in 2303? Would they get classed separately? I know the author hates overusing GoIs, but I'm sure gamers against weed got bored with some of their pranks before finishing them…
Congrats. This might've been a stepping stone to a 001 proposal, but it's a darn fine scip on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is quite interesting, but the middle sections dragged on without much that really intrigued me. Perhaps it's because I've read several of them in a relatively short period of time, or because they are so visually distinctive, but I feel like I've reached a tipping point where articles giving long tables of various manifestations of phenomenon that have some vague thematic or narrative connection (see also, SCP-2747 and SCP-2188) have stopped being novel and have started being tedious.

The letter, though, was what really sold me. It revealed some new information, but mostly it was the wording itself that really appealed to me and actually helped to bring home why this was unsettling.

One thing that stuck out at me was the description of the treatise by a "ninth century AD" author. Given that Avicenna died in the eleventh century, and Thomas Aquinas lived and died in the thirteenth century, I assume that this was some sort of anomaly, rather than a flub. If that's the case, it sounds like a very interesting, but presumably as-yet-unwritten object. However, the fact that this passes without comment in the article is a bit unusual.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A little pedantic, but shouldn't the Australian Space Program say Interstellar Probe instead of Interstellar Satellite ? There's currently a conceptual plan for an Interstellar Probe mission, but there's really no mechanism for it to actually brake as it passes through the system.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ciudad Encrucijada is a fictional abandoned city, located in the real-life Argentinian province of Rio Negro.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, nice catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just pointing out that there's a casual in-line reference to SCP-2798…

… from a few weeks before it was posted…

Kalinin, what're you up to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you tried just PM'ing Kalinin?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since this has now dropped off the front page of recently created, I'm going to request once again that someone answer this question. If no one responds soon, then I'll give up on trying to add this one to my map.

item.title: I'm clearly missing something...
item.content:encoded:
(nevermind :) )

Edit: Though I'm still missing the connection between the different abandoned ideas. It FEELS very strongly that there is a link; it's like when you know you KNOW the word you're trying to think of, it's on the tip of your tongue, but you can't quite get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like the shout-out to ST:TNG with the Argus Array. :-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this:
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Man_on_the_Threshold
A short story by Jorge Luis Borges.
But it doesn't seem to correspond to the skip…?
A search on the SCP website doesn't seem to get anything relevant either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm wondering about that too. Is that something I have to Google on my own? If not, there might be a broken link or something.

Other than that, I like the story. It's kind of intriguing that there's something classified too highly for the reader to see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also is this a real abandoned city? I'm having trouble finding it on the map.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean there is an SCP based on it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I completely agree with TwistedGears: strong opening, but then goes on and on, and the last addenddum doesn't do much to the skip.
OTOH, the starting idea is good and original, the writing flawless, the picture excellent, and the general challenge met.
I also like the idea that in the expected chaos of 'here are dreams that never came true', there is still some organisation as to which theme goes on which floor.

Reading up about "The Man at the Threshold" was remarkably satisfying.

Does this refer to something I should get, but don't? Skip? Tale? IRL?

Upvote, but a reasonable one, not a wild enthusiastic one!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article speaks to me very strongly. Usually the articles I read trigger a rational reaction. I'll sit back and think about the words, the concept and the execution and then decide whether or not I like it. It's very rarely that an article evokes an immediate emotional reaction. This article manages to do that, one I can't really explain or analyze, and for that it deserves my eternal upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, you upvoted SCP-2102, so there's ways around that stance. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The error is mine in never elaborating that stance despite numerous threats to do so. The short version is that I'm against defined, canonical GoIs showing up in articles as a general rule. This is a one-shot thing invented for the purpose of this specific narrative. I am of course going to violate that rule myself in the near future, so that goes to show you what happens when one holds beliefs of any sort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Superconducting Supercollider, ironically enough, is the only one of those that is a real thing. So no, not an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a running theme of some sort between the examples

Yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't the superconducting supercollider already an SCP? Maybe an appropriate cross-reference could be made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I agree with TG over the ending, I do enjoy the entries in the middle (particularly those under artistic works, religion and philosophy). They make me curious as to what could have been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a running theme of some sort between the examples (like in Faulkner's Last Manuscript)? If that's the case, I don't seem to be getting it.

Solid article, but I concur with kaktus that it lacks a bit of a punch in the middle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reading up about "The Man at the Threshold" was remarkably satisfying. Intended or otherwise, it's a really solid way to wrap this whole thing up.

The example logs in the middle did tend to draw on, and as a whole, didn't explore the concept as thoroughly as I thought you might. It all came off as a bit too sterilized, without some of the visceral ideas that the premise could support. To compare this to "To End All Wars", that article is full of examples of the "ends" of an idea, while this one made me wonder why similar ideas weren't included (in terms of theme, not necessarily content). Not to say the examples used were bad ones, obviously. I still upvoted it.

The ending was good, though. The concept is brilliant, the setting is sublime, the image is top notch, and the title is perfect. Tower of Silence. I love it. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is where all my fanfic ideas go.

A couple thoughts. The article itself isn't tremendously exciting, but at the same time, it's one of those abstract, nebulous skips where the satisfaction comes in being able to sit back and go, "Yeah, I get it." I did feel like there were maybe too many examples; after a couple regular and a couple anomalous ones, I had the gist.

Then I was going to complain about the "hey, this is secretly an initiation for an MTF" addendum, but by the end, it told a good enough story to recast the entire article in a new light. This ends up being overall a very good skip. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the opening to this was really strong. The anomaly itself, on a conceptual level, really appeals to me. But then it just kinda keeps on going, none of the listed things really did a whole lot for me so I felt the need to skim, but I was afraid of missing something if I did. Ending it on a note, because fucking yay that trope yet again, that explains "hey don't use the thing" just… kills this for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was very enjoyable.
The concept is neat, and the table of observations was also really fun. The final addenda was what sold the article for me though. "You leave that shit in the tower."

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Detailed information of this proposal ceases sometime around the launch of a research mission bound for the Saturnian moon of Iapetus.

I see what you did here. I like it, and I had to do a double-take to make sure it was referring to what I thought it was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As usual, both complex and sublime. Although I thought you were opposed to the concept of there being groups of interest to the Foundation. I must have misunderstood you.

item.title: AUTHOR POST
item.content:encoded: Original photograph was taken by flickr user Thomas Hobbs, and may be accessed here. This image appears under the CC BY-SA 2.0 license. Under the terms of that license, disclosing that the image was manipulated on 1/12/16.
